items.misconception: 8xy, by combining all terms regardless of variable.
items.random_wrong: 9xy, by adding all the numbers and merging the letters.
items.why: Students treat different variables as interchangeable, missing that only terms with the same variable (and power) can be combined.

items.correct: 12 — the product of two negatives is positive.
items.misconception: -12, because there's a negative sign in the calculation.
items.random_wrong: -7, because the negatives are being combined.
items.why: Students preserve negative signs out of habit and don't internalise that an even number of negative factors produces a positive product.

items.correct: 18 — division and multiplication share the same precedence; work left to right (6 x 3).
items.misconception: 2, because in BIDMAS multiplication comes before division (12 / 6).
items.random_wrong: 7, because you do 12 / 2 = 6 and then add 3 with the multiplication symbol meaning 'and'.
items.why: Students treat BIDMAS as a strict order (M before D), missing that multiplication and division have equal priority and are evaluated left to right.
Correct answer: 2. “10” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: 21. “18” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is 48. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
